message.content:
You are an independent blind verifier (verifier A) for the hc-assay hardening campaign at /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay (branch harden/pass-10). Decide TRUE_POSITIVE (genuine production-readiness defect to fix) or FALSE_POSITIVE (false/already-mitigated/out-of-scope-by-design/not real). Out-of-scope: dataset-agnostic opaque inputs; optional import-guarded backends; intentionally-committed scrubbed transcripts (but a NEW leaked PII/secret class IS in scope); on-box data-sovereignty; documented honest-scope. Read real code and REPRODUCE where testable (cd /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay && source .venv/bin/activate && python ...; "python -m pytest"; gh). Judge ONLY SEC-10-1.

FINDING SEC-10-1 (high): libpq PGHOSTADDR/PGHOST/PGSERVICE env vars bypass the loopback guard
Location: src/assay_engine/persistence/checkpoint.py + _local.py require_local_uri
Claim: require_local_uri validates the connection URI/DSN string, but libpq ALSO reads PGHOST/PGHOSTADDR/PGPORT/PGSERVICE environment variables which OVERRIDE or supply the host when the DSN omits it (or even when it doesn't, for PGHOSTADDR). So the checkpointer's data-sovereignty guard can be bypassed: set PGHOSTADDR=<off-box IP> (or PGHOST, or a PGSERVICE pointing off-box) and graph state leaves the box despite the guard passing on a loopback-looking DSN. VERIFY: does checkpoint.py / the psycopg ConnectionPool honor libpq env vars? Does require_local_uri or any code neutralize/validate PGHOST*/PGSERVICE? Is the connection string fully-specified (host present) so libpq env can't fill it — or could PGHOSTADDR still override? Assess realistically (libpq precedence: explicit conn param > PGHOSTADDR/PGHOST env).

attachment.content:
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.content:
1	"""Durable run-state checkpointer — local, data-sovereign (ADR-0003).
2	
3	The orchestration graph persists its full state (current phase, gate decisions, locked
4	hypotheses, verdicts) through a checkpointer so a run can interrupt at a human-approval gate
5	and resume later. That state holds the entire analysis, so the store is **on-box only**
6	(ADR-0003); a SaaS checkpointer is disqualified.
7	
8	This module is the LangGraph checkpointer factory, ported faithfully from the prior
9	platform's hardened implementation and generalized (dataset-agnostic ``ASSAY_*`` env, no
10	config-file coupling — see ADR-0006). The hardened behaviors are deliberately preserved
11	because each prevents a real production failure:
12	
13	- a **self-healing ConnectionPool** (not a raw connection): when Postgres goes away — node
14	  sleep, restart, idle timeout, a network blip — a cached raw connection is permanently
15	  broken and every later checkpoint op raises, i.e. durability is lost exactly during the
16	  interrupt/resume the checkpointer exists to support. The pool runs a liveness check on
17	  checkout and replaces dead connections;
18	- a **session advisory lock** serializing first-time schema setup across *processes*, run on
19	  the *same* connection as the DDL (a pool would otherwise check out a different connection,
20	  leaving the lock ineffective);
21	- a **once-per-process-per-conn guard** (``setup()`` is idempotent but re-locking + replaying
22	  the migration query on every call is wasteful), with an in-process lock making the
23	  check-and-set atomic so concurrent threads don't both run setup;
24	- **credential redaction**: a backend exception (psycopg/psycopg_pool) can embed the full
25	  ``user:password`` URI/DSN in its own message, so the conn string is scrubbed precisely and
26	  the exception text via URI-userinfo + DSN-password backstops; the redacted message is then
27	  raised *outside* the exception handler, so the credential-bearing original is retained as
28	  neither ``__cause__`` nor ``__context__`` (a logger walking ``__context__`` cannot
29	  re-expose it);
30	- a single shared **atexit** pool-cleanup handler (one per process, not one per call) so the
31	  pool's worker thread is joined before interpreter finalization.
32	
33	LangGraph/psycopg are optional (the ``persistence`` extra) and imported lazily; the
34	credential-redaction and connection-resolution logic is pure and unit-tested offline.
35	"""
36	
37	from __future__ import annotations
38	
39	import atexit
40	import os
41	import re
42	import threading
43	from typing import Any, cast
44	from urllib.parse import urlsplit
45	
46	from assay_engine._envparse import int_env
47	from assay_engine._local import require_local_uri
48	
49	_DEFAULT_PG = "postgresql://localhost:5432/assay"
50	
51	# Arbitrary fixed key for the Postgres session advisory lock that serializes checkpoint
52	# schema migrations across concurrent initializers. The value is irrelevant beyond being a
53	# stable per-logical-schema constant; "ASSY" as an int.
54	_MIGRATION_LOCK_KEY = 0x4153_5359
55	
56	# Upper bound (milliseconds) on how long the cross-process advisory lock may be waited for
57	# during schema bootstrap. A stuck/crashed peer that still holds a live PG session on the lock
58	# must NOT be able to hang bootstrap forever (#129); lock_timeout aborts the wait. 0 disables
59	# the bound (block indefinitely, the pre-#129 behavior). Read fresh on each bootstrap (#F-033) so
60	# an env var set after import still takes effect; the module constant is the default + the value
61	# tests monkeypatch.
62	_MIGRATION_LOCK_TIMEOUT_MS = max(0, int_env("ASSAY_MIGRATION_LOCK_TIMEOUT_MS", "30000"))
63	
64	
65	def _migration_lock_timeout_ms() -> int:
66	    """The advisory-lock wait bound, re-read from the env each call (#F-033).
67	
68	    Falls back to the import-time module constant when the env var is unset, so a test that
69	    monkeypatches ``_MIGRATION_LOCK_TIMEOUT_MS`` still drives the value.
70	    """
71	    raw = os.environ.get("ASSAY_MIGRATION_LOCK_TIMEOUT_MS")
72	    if raw is None or raw == "":
73	        return _MIGRATION_LOCK_TIMEOUT_MS
74	    return max(0, int_env("ASSAY_MIGRATION_LOCK_TIMEOUT_MS", "30000"))  # names the var on error
75	
76	
77	# Brief meta-lock guarding the process-local registries below (atexit registration, the open-pool
78	# list, the per-conn lock map, the pool cache). It is held ONLY for in-memory check-and-set — it
79	# is NEVER held across blocking cross-process I/O (pool.connection(), the advisory-lock wait, or
80	# setup() DDL). That blocking work is serialized per conn_str instead (#129), so a stuck advisory
81	# lock on one database cannot block initialization of an unrelated one.
82	_init_lock = threading.Lock()
83	
84	# Per-conn_str bootstrap locks: only same-conn_str initializers serialize; different databases
85	# bootstrap concurrently. Created lazily under _init_lock.
86	_CONN_INIT_LOCKS: dict[str, threading.Lock] = {}
87	
88	# Live pools cached by conn_str so the factory reuses one self-healing pool (and its worker
89	# thread) per database rather than opening a fresh pool on every call (#144).
90	_POOLS_BY_CONN: dict[str, Any] = {}
91	
92	# Pools opened by the factory, closed once at process exit via a single shared atexit handler
93	# (registering one handler per call would accumulate handlers across repeated factory calls).
94	_OPEN_POOLS: list[Any] = []
95	_atexit_registered = False
96	
97	# Strips ``://[REDACTED]@`` (URI userinfo) from any URI-like substring. '/' is allowed
98	# inside the userinfo so a password containing '/' is still redacted; only '@' (the userinfo
99	# terminator) and whitespace bound the match.
100	_CREDS_RE = re.compile(r"://[REDACTED]@\s]+@")
101	# Strips a libpq keyword/value DSN password token (``password=[REDACTED] / ``pgpassword=...``),
102	# handling quoted values that may contain spaces.
103	_DSN_PW_RE = re.compile(r"(?i)\b(password|pgpassword)\s*=\s*('[^']*'|\"[^\"]*\"|\S+)")
104	
105	
106	def _sanitize_conn_str(conn_str: str) -> str:
107	    """Return ``scheme://host[:port]/path`` with any ``user:password`` userinfo stripped.
108	
109	    Robust to malformed userinfo: a password containing ``/``/``#`` makes ``urlsplit``'s
110	    ``.port`` raise ``ValueError`` on access, so the whole parse (including attribute access)
111	    is guarded and falls back to the generic userinfo-strip regex / a constant placeholder.
112	    """
113	    try:
114	        u = urlsplit(conn_str)
115	        host = u.hostname or ""
116	        port = u.port
117	        scheme = u.scheme
118	        path = u.path
119	    except ValueError:
120	        return _CREDS_RE.sub("://", conn_str)
121	    if not host:
122	        return _CREDS_RE.sub("://", conn_str) if scheme else "<store>"
123	    if ":" in host:  # IPv6 literal — urlsplit drops the brackets; restore them
124	        host = f"[{host}]"
125	    netloc = f"{host}:{port}" if port else host
126	    return f"{scheme}://{netloc}{path}" if scheme else "<store>"
127	
128	
129	def redact_creds(text: str, conn_str: str = "") -> str:
130	    """Remove credentials a backend exception may embed in its message text.
131	
132	    Layers, in order of precision:
133	
134	    1. exact replacement of the known ``conn_str`` (handles any password characters, incl.
135	       ``/`` and spaces) — this is the precise layer and covers the real connection string;
136	    2. a generic URI-userinfo strip (``://[REDACTED]@`` → ``://``) as a backstop for any
137	       *other* URI in the text;
138	    3. a libpq DSN ``password=[REDACTED] strip for the keyword/value form.
139	
140	    Layers 2–3 are best-effort for *secondary* endpoints (e.g. a replica DSN) the caller did
141	    not pass as ``conn_str``.
142	
143	    Precision caveat (pass 5, #H-023): layer 1 is an EXACT-substring replacement, so it scrubs the
144	    primary connection string precisely only when that string appears verbatim in ``text``. If a
145	    backend reformats it (re-quoting, reordering DSN keywords, percent-encoding the password) the
146	    verbatim match misses and only the layers-2–3 regex backstops apply — which still strip
147	    ``://[REDACTED]@`` userinfo and ``password=[REDACTED] tokens, but are not guaranteed exhaustive for
148	    every backend message shape. It is "precise when the conn string appears verbatim", not
149	    "always precise".
150	    """
151	    if conn_str:
152	        text = text.replace(conn_str, _sanitize_conn_str(conn_str))
153	    text = _CREDS_RE.sub("://", text)
154	    return _DSN_PW_RE.sub(r"\1=***", text)
155	
156	
157	def get_postgres_connection_string() -> str:
158	    """Resolve the Postgres connection string (``ASSAY_POSTGRES_URL`` env → loopback default).
159	
160	    The env path bypasses any external config validation, so loopback is enforced here at the
161	    point of use (ADR-0003 defense-in-depth): graph state must not leave the box.
162	    """
163	    url = os.environ.get("ASSAY_POSTGRES_URL") or _DEFAULT_PG
164	    return require_local_uri(url, what="checkpointer connection")
165	
166	
167	def _safe_close(pool: Any) -> None:
168	    """Close one pool, swallowing errors so a single failing ``close()`` does not abort
169	    cleanup of the remaining pools at interpreter exit (``close()`` calls a C ``finish()``
170	    that can raise)."""
171	    try:
172	        pool.close()
173	    except Exception:
174	        pass
175	
176	
177	def _close_all_pools() -> None:
178	    # Snapshot under the meta-lock before iterating (#F-020): _OPEN_POOLS is appended under
179	    # _init_lock elsewhere, and on a free-threaded (GIL-less) build a concurrent list.append
180	    # reallocation during iteration is undefined behavior. _safe_close never takes _init_lock, so
181	    # closing outside the lock cannot deadlock.
182	    with _init_lock:
183	        pools = list(_OPEN_POOLS)
184	    for p in pools:
185	        _safe_close(p)
186	
187	
188	def _register_pool_cleanup(pool: Any) -> None:
189	    global _atexit_registered
190	    with _init_lock:
191	        _OPEN_POOLS.append(pool)
192	        if not _atexit_registered:
193	            atexit.register(_close_all_pools)
194	            _atexit_registered = True
195	
196	
197	def _conn_init_lock(conn_str: str) -> threading.Lock:
198	    """Return the per-conn_str bootstrap lock, creating it under the brief meta-lock (#129)."""
199	    with _init_lock:
200	        lock = _CONN_INIT_LOCKS.get(conn_str)
201	        if lock is None:
202	            lock = _CONN_INIT_LOCKS[conn_str] = threading.Lock()
203	        return lock
204	
205	
206	def _cached_pool(conn_str: str) -> Any:
207	    with _init_lock:
208	        return _POOLS_BY_CONN.get(conn_str)
209	
210	
211	def _acquire_migration_lock(conn: Any) -> None:
212	    """Acquire the cross-process schema-migration advisory lock with a bounded wait (#129).
213	
214	    ``lock_timeout`` aborts the ``pg_advisory_lock`` wait if a peer holds it past the deadline,
215	    so a stuck/crashed initializer cannot hang bootstrap indefinitely; the resulting error
216	    propagates as a redacted ``RuntimeError`` like any other backend failure. set_config is used
217	    (not ``SET``) so the value can be safely parameterized.
218	    """
219	    timeout_ms = _migration_lock_timeout_ms()
220	    if timeout_ms:
221	        conn.execute("SELECT set_config('lock_timeout', %s, false)", (str(timeout_ms),))
222	    conn.execute("SELECT pg_advisory_lock(%s)", (_MIGRATION_LOCK_KEY,))
223	
224	
225	def get_checkpointer(use_memory: bool = False) -> Any:
226	    """Return a configured LangGraph checkpointer.
227	
228	    ``use_memory=True`` returns an in-memory saver (tests / no-Postgres). Otherwise a
229	    Postgres-backed saver over a self-healing pool, with advisory-locked one-time schema
230	    setup. Raises ``RuntimeError`` (with credentials redacted) if the backend is unreachable
231	    or the extra is not installed.
232	    """
233	    try:
234	        if use_memory:
235	            from langgraph.checkpoint.memory import MemorySaver
236	
237	            return MemorySaver()
238	        from langgraph.checkpoint.postgres import PostgresSaver
239	        from psycopg.rows import dict_row
240	        from psycopg_pool import ConnectionPool
241	    except ImportError as exc:  # pragma: no cover - exercised only without the extra
242	        raise RuntimeError(
243	            "Postgres checkpointer requires the 'persistence' extra "
244	            "(langgraph-checkpoint-postgres, psycopg[binary,pool]) — not installed"
245	        ) from exc
246	
247	    conn_str = get_postgres_connection_string()
248	
249	    # Fast path: reuse a live, self-healing pool for this conn_str rather than opening a new pool
250	    # (and its background worker thread) on every call (#144). The liveness check on checkout
251	    # replaces dead connections, so a cached pool stays valid across transient outages.
252	    cached = _cached_pool(conn_str)
253	    if cached is not None:
254	        return PostgresSaver(cast(Any, cached))
255	
256	    # Serialize bootstrap PER conn_str — NOT process-globally (#129). Unrelated databases
257	    # initialize concurrently, and a stuck advisory lock on one conn_str can never block init of
258	    # another. The brief meta-lock (_init_lock) is only taken inside the helpers, never held
259	    # across the blocking advisory-lock wait / DDL below.
260	    with _conn_init_lock(conn_str):
261	        cached = _cached_pool(conn_str)  # another thread may have built it while we waited
262	        if cached is not None:
263	            return PostgresSaver(cast(Any, cached))
264	
265	        failure: str | None = None
266	        pool: Any = None
267	        try:
268	            # Pool bounds are env-tunable (#F-040): the reasoning seam already runs 8 worker
269	            # threads, so a fixed max_size=8 can be saturated under concurrent reasoning+checkpoint
270	            # load on larger hardware. connect_timeout bounds an individual TCP connect so a
271	            # black-holed host fails fast instead of hanging bootstrap for the OS default.
272	            min_size = max(0, int_env("ASSAY_POOL_MIN_SIZE", "1"))
273	            max_size = max(min_size or 1, int_env("ASSAY_POOL_MAX_SIZE", "8"))
274	            connect_timeout = max(1, int_env("ASSAY_POOL_CONNECT_TIMEOUT", "10"))
275	            pool = ConnectionPool(
276	                conn_str,
277	                min_size=min_size,
278	                max_size=max_size,
279	                kwargs={
280	                    "autocommit": True,
281	                    "prepare_threshold": 0,
282	                    "row_factory": dict_row,
283	                    "connect_timeout": connect_timeout,
284	                },
285	                check=ConnectionPool.check_connection,  # liveness check on checkout (self-healing)
286	                open=True,
287	            )
288	            _register_pool_cleanup(pool)
289	            # Bootstrap the schema once per process per conn_str. The advisory lock and the DDL
290	            # must run on the SAME connection (pg_advisory_lock is session-scoped); a pool-backed
291	            # setup() would lock one connection and DDL on another, leaving the lock ineffective.
292	            with pool.connection() as conn:
293	                _acquire_migration_lock(conn)  # bounded wait so a stuck peer can't hang us (#129)
294	                try:
295	                    # row_factory=dict_row makes this connection dict-rowed at runtime
296	                    # (what PostgresSaver requires); the cast asserts that runtime truth
297	                    # the type system can't infer from the pool kwargs.
298	                    PostgresSaver(cast(Any, conn)).setup()  # DDL on the locked connection
299	                finally:
300	                    # Best-effort unlock; the lock auto-releases when the connection
301	                    # returns to the pool, and raising here would mask a setup() error.
302	                    try:
303	                        conn.execute("SELECT pg_advisory_unlock(%s)", (_MIGRATION_LOCK_KEY,))
304	                    except Exception:
305	                        pass
306	                    # Reset the session lock_timeout GUC (#G-002): the autocommit connection set it
307	                    # at SESSION scope for the bootstrap, then returns to the pool and is reused for
308	                    # all later checkpoint reads/writes — which would otherwise silently inherit a
309	                    # non-default lock_timeout the operator never configured for normal ops.
310	                    try:
311	                        conn.execute("RESET lock_timeout")
312	                    except Exception:
313	                        pass
314	            with _init_lock:
315	                _POOLS_BY_CONN[conn_str] = pool  # cache for reuse on later calls (#144)
316	            return PostgresSaver(cast(Any, pool))  # dict-rowed pool (row_factory=dict_row)
317	        except Exception as exc:
318	            # Build the redacted message here, but raise it OUTSIDE the handler (below) so the
319	            # original — credential-bearing — exception is retained as neither __cause__ NOR
320	            # __context__ (a logger walking __context__ would otherwise re-expose the password).
321	            failure = (
322	                f"Postgres checkpointer connection failed (is Postgres running on "
323	                f"{_sanitize_conn_str(conn_str)}?): {redact_creds(str(exc), conn_str)}. "
324	                "For tests without Postgres, use get_checkpointer(use_memory=True)."
325	            )
326	            # The pool may have opened before bootstrap failed — close it and drop it from the
327	            # cleanup registry so a failed init does not leak the connection/worker thread (#105).
328	            if pool is not None:
329	                _safe_close(pool)
330	                with _init_lock:
331	                    if pool in _OPEN_POOLS:
332	                        _OPEN_POOLS.remove(pool)
333	        raise RuntimeError(failure)
334	
335	
336	def configured_checkpointer(use_memory: bool | None = None) -> Any:
337	    """Return the checkpointer the environment selects.
338	
339	    ``use_memory`` (or ``ASSAY_CHECKPOINT_BACKEND=memory``) chooses the in-memory saver;
340	    otherwise Postgres.
341	    """
342	    if use_memory is None:
343	        use_memory = os.environ.get("ASSAY_CHECKPOINT_BACKEND", "postgres").lower() == "memory"
344	    return get_checkpointer(use_memory=use_memory)
345

content.content:
1	"""Data-sovereignty helper: enforce that network endpoints are loopback-only (ADR-0003).
2	
3	Every engine component that takes a host/URL for an on-box service (local model runtime,
4	tracing collector, vector store, checkpoint database) routes it through here so a non-local
5	address fails loud rather than silently shipping data off the machine.
6	"""
7	
8	from __future__ import annotations
9	
10	import ipaddress
11	import re
12	from urllib.parse import parse_qs, urlparse
13	
14	# A libpq keyword/value token: ``key = value`` with optional whitespace around ``=`` (libpq
15	# permits it) and an optionally single/double-quoted value (which may contain spaces).
16	_DSN_TOKEN_RE = re.compile(r"(\w+)\s*=\s*('[^']*'|\"[^\"]*\"|\S+)")
17	
18	
19	class NonLocalEndpointError(RuntimeError):
20	    """Raised when a configured endpoint is not loopback (would leave the machine)."""
21	
22	
23	def is_loopback_host(host: str | None) -> bool:
24	    """True only for genuine loopback hosts.
25	
26	    Parses the host as an IP and tests the loopback network (127.0.0.0/8, ::1), so a public
27	    name that merely *starts with* ``127.`` (e.g. ``127.0.0.1.evil.com``) is correctly
28	    rejected. The literal ``localhost`` is the only accepted non-IP host.
29	    """
30	    if not host:
31	        return False
32	    h = host.strip().lower()
33	    if h == "localhost":
34	        return True
35	    try:
36	        return ipaddress.ip_address(h.strip("[]")).is_loopback
37	    except ValueError:
38	        return False
39	
40	
41	def is_local_socket_path(host: str | None) -> bool:
42	    """True for a libpq Unix-domain-socket host — a leading-'/' absolute directory (#K-SEC-1).
43	
44	    libpq accepts ``host=/var/run/postgresql`` (or the query form ``?host=/tmp``) to connect over
45	    a local Unix-domain socket — a connection that NEVER touches the network, so it is strictly
46	    more data-sovereign than the TCP loopback the guard already permits. A single leading '/' is a
47	    local path; a Windows UNC prefix (two leading separators, e.g. ``//server`` or ``/\\server``)
48	    resolves to a remote SMB share and must still be rejected.
49	    """
50	    if not host:
51	        return False
52	    h = host.strip()
53	    # A comma means libpq MULTI-HOST (e.g. "/tmp,evil.com") — never a single socket dir. Reject so
54	    # the leading-'/' check can't wave through an off-box host hidden after the comma (#K-SEC-9-1).
55	    # Callers split multi-host and validate each element; a leaf reaching here must be one host.
56	    if "," in h:
57	        return False
58	    return h.startswith("/") and not h.startswith("//") and not h.startswith("/\\")
59	
60	
61	def require_loopback_url(url: str, *, what: str) -> str:
62	    """Return ``url`` if its host is loopback; else raise :class:`NonLocalEndpointError`."""
63	    host = urlparse(url).hostname
64	    if not is_loopback_host(host):
65	        raise NonLocalEndpointError(
66	            f"{what} must be loopback-only (ADR-0003 data sovereignty); got host {host!r} "
67	            f"from {url!r}"
68	        )
69	    return url
70	
71	
72	def require_loopback_host(host: str, *, what: str) -> str:
73	    """Return ``host`` if it is loopback; else raise. (``0.0.0.0`` binds all interfaces and
74	    is correctly rejected — it would expose data off-box.)"""
75	    if not is_loopback_host(host):
76	        raise NonLocalEndpointError(
77	            f"{what} must be a loopback host (ADR-0003 data sovereignty); got {host!r}"
78	        )
79	    return host
80	
81	
82	def _authority_hosts(netloc: str) -> list[str]:
83	    """Every host in a URI authority, supporting libpq's comma-separated multi-host form.
84	
85	    ``urlparse().hostname`` returns only the *first* host of a multi-host authority
86	    (``host1:port1,host2:port2``), so a remote second host would slip past a single-host
87	    check (audit #D3). Each host may carry its own ``:port`` and IPv6 hosts are bracketed.
88	
89	    Userinfo is stripped at the **first** ``@`` — matching libpq, which (unlike ``urlparse``,
90	    which uses the last ``@``) treats the first ``@`` as the userinfo delimiter. A string like
91	    ``[REDACTED]:5432@localhost`` is therefore read as host ``evil.com`` here, not
92	    ``localhost`` (audit #D5). Any host element still containing ``@`` after that is ambiguous
93	    and is returned verbatim so the caller's loopback check fails it closed.
94	    """
95	    if "@" in netloc:
96	        netloc = netloc.split("@", 1)[1]
97	    hosts: list[str] = []
98	    for part in netloc.split(","):
99	        p = part.strip()
100	        if not p:
101	            continue
102	        if p.startswith("["):  # [ipv6] or [ipv6]:port
103	            end = p.find("]")
104	            hosts.append(p[1:end] if end != -1 else p[1:])
105	        else:
106	            hosts.append(p.rsplit(":", 1)[0] if ":" in p else p)
107	    return hosts
108	
109	
110	def require_local_uri(uri: str, *, what: str) -> str:
111	    """Return ``uri`` if it is local (ADR-0003), else raise :class:`NonLocalEndpointError`.
112	
113	    Three forms are recognized, validated against what the real client (libpq for postgres,
114	    urllib for http, sqlite for files) actually connects to — not merely what ``urlparse``
115	    reports, since the two can disagree:
116	
117	    - **URI with an authority** (``scheme://host…`` or scheme-relative ``//host…``): *every*
118	      host in the authority must be loopback (incl. each of a comma-separated multi-host
119	      authority, #D3), AND any ``host``/``hostaddr`` query parameter that libpq would use to
120	      override the authority host (#D2). ``hostaddr`` is checked independently of ``host``.
121	    - **libpq keyword/value DSN** (no authority but ``key=value`` tokens): ``host``/``hostaddr``
122	      must be loopback, tolerating libpq's whitespace/quoting around ``=`` (#P1, #D1).
123	    - **bare path** (no authority, no ``=``): a local file/dir — but a Windows UNC path
124	      (``\\\\server\\share``) is rejected as remote.
125	
126	    Inputs that cannot be parsed safely fail closed (rejected), never accepted.
127	    """
128	    try:
129	        parsed = urlparse(uri)
130	        netloc = parsed.netloc
131	        query = parsed.query
132	    except ValueError:
133	        # e.g. a malformed IPv6 authority — we cannot determine the host, so refuse.
134	        raise NonLocalEndpointError(
135	            f"{what}: endpoint could not be parsed safely; rejecting (ADR-0003)"
136	        ) from None
137	
138	    if netloc:
139	        # A backslash never legitimately appears in a URI authority; a WHATWG-normalizing
140	        # client (e.g. some HTTP stacks) treats '\' as '/', so it could read a different host
141	        # than urlparse. Refuse rather than risk that differential (audit #D6, defense-in-depth).
142	        if "\\" in netloc:
143	            raise NonLocalEndpointError(
144	                f"{what}: backslash in URI authority is not permitted (ADR-0003); rejecting"
145	            )
146	        for h in _authority_hosts(netloc):
147	            # A residual '@' means the authority is ambiguous between this parser and libpq —
148	            # fail closed (audit #D5).
149	            if "@" in h or not is_loopback_host(h):
150	                raise NonLocalEndpointError(
151	                    f"{what} must be a local store (ADR-0003 data sovereignty); authority "
152	                    f"names non-loopback host {h!r}"
153	                )
154	        for key in ("host", "hostaddr"):
155	            for val in parse_qs(query).get(key, []):
156	                # libpq allows a COMMA-SEPARATED multi-host value; split and validate EVERY element
157	                # (#K-SEC-9-1) — else "/tmp,evil.com" slipped through because the whole string
158	                # looked like a socket path and the off-box host after the comma was never checked.
159	                for h in (p.strip() for p in val.split(",")):
160	                    if h and not is_loopback_host(h) and not is_local_socket_path(h):
161	                        raise NonLocalEndpointError(
162	                            f"{what} must be a local store (ADR-0003 data sovereignty); URI query "
163	                            f"{key}={h!r} points off-box"
164	                        )
165	        return uri
166	
167	    if "=" in uri:  # libpq keyword/value DSN (no URI authority)
168	        for match in _DSN_TOKEN_RE.finditer(uri):
169	            if match.group(1).strip().lower() in {"host", "hostaddr"}:
170	                raw = match.group(2).strip().strip("'\"")
171	                # Split libpq's comma-separated multi-host and validate each element (#K-SEC-9-1).
172	                for h in (p.strip() for p in raw.split(",")):
173	                    if h and not is_loopback_host(h) and not is_local_socket_path(h):
174	                        raise NonLocalEndpointError(
175	                            f"{what} must be a local store (ADR-0003 data sovereignty); DSN names "
176	                            f"non-loopback host {h!r}"
177	                        )
178	        return uri
179	
180	    stripped = uri.lstrip()
181	    # Windows treats ANY two leading separators (in any mix of '\' and '/') as a UNC prefix
182	    # to a remote SMB share — \\srv, //srv, /\srv, \/srv all resolve off-box (audit #D6).
183	    # (The pure '//host' form is already caught by the authority branch above; covering it
184	    # here too keeps the branch self-contained.)
185	    if len(stripped) >= 2 and stripped[0] in "\\/" and stripped[1] in "\\/":
186	        raise NonLocalEndpointError(
187	            f"{what} must be a local store (ADR-0003 data sovereignty); UNC-style path "
188	            f"{uri!r} is a remote share"
189	        )
190	    return uri  # bare path / dir store
191

content.text:
The finding claims libpq env vars (PGHOSTADDR/PGHOST/PGSERVICE) can bypass the loopback guard. The guard validates only the conn string/DSN, not the environment. The key question is whether the connection string passed to `ConnectionPool` is fully specified such that libpq env vars cannot fill or override the host.

Let me analyze libpq precedence carefully:
- Explicit connection parameters (in the DSN/URI) take precedence over env vars.
- But env vars fill in parameters NOT specified in the connection string.

The default DSN is `postgresql://localhost:5432/assay`. Here `host=localhost` is explicit. The critical libpq nuance: **PGHOSTADDR** sets `hostaddr`, which is a SEPARATE parameter from `host`. When `host` is given but `hostaddr` is NOT given in the conn string, libpq will read PGHOSTADDR env. If PGHOSTADDR is set, libpq connects to that IP address (using `host` only for SSL/auth name verification).

Let me verify this is reproducible.

content.text: Confirmed: nothing in the codebase neutralizes, clears, or validates PGHOST/PGHOSTADDR/PGPORT/PGSERVICE environment variables. The guard only inspects the DSN/URI string. Let me verify the libpq precedence claim empirically with psycopg's conninfo parser, which mirrors libpq.

input.command: cd /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay && source .venv/bin/activate && python -c "import psycopg; print(psycopg.__version__)" 2>&1 | head -5

cd /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay && source .venv/bin/activate && python - <<'EOF'
from psycopg.conninfo import conninfo_to_dict, make_conninfo
import os

dsn = "postgresql://localhost:5432/assay"
print("== parsed dict (no env applied by conninfo_to_dict) ==")
print(conninfo_to_dict(dsn))

# psycopg uses libpq to actually resolve. Let's see what libpq resolves with PGHOSTADDR set.
# Use psycopg.connect's conninfo resolution via the C lib: conn_info expansion happens at connect.
# We can inspect via libpq's PQconninfoParse-like behavior using psycopg's pq module.
from psycopg import pq
print("\n== libpq version ==", pq.version())

# Build the actual connect string and ask libpq what defaults it fills.
os.environ["PGHOSTADDR"] = "203.0.113.66"
ci = make_conninfo(dsn)
print("\nmake_conninfo:", ci)
opts = pq.Conninfo.get_defaults() if hasattr(pq.Conninfo,'get_defaults') else None
EOF

content.text: The DSN specifies `host=localhost` and `port=5432` but does NOT specify `hostaddr`. Now let me demonstrate the actual libpq behavior: with `host` explicitly set but `hostaddr` NOT in the conn string, libpq reads PGHOSTADDR from env and connects to that IP. I'll prove libpq picks up PGHOSTADDR by attempting a connection and observing where it actually tries to connect.

input.command:
cd /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay && source .venv/bin/activate && python - <<'EOF'
import os, psycopg
from psycopg import pq

dsn = "postgresql://localhost:5432/assay"

# Point PGHOSTADDR at an off-box, non-routable test IP (TEST-NET-3, RFC5737) on a port
# that will refuse fast / time out. We just want to see WHERE libpq tries to connect.
os.environ["PGHOSTADDR"] = "203.0.113.66"
os.environ.pop("PGHOST", None)
os.environ.pop("PGSERVICE", None)

try:
    psycopg.connect(dsn, connect_timeout=2)
    print("CONNECTED (unexpected)")
except Exception as e:
    msg = str(e)
    print("ERROR MESSAGE:\n", msg)
    if "203.0.113.66" in msg:
        print("\n>>> PROOF: libpq attempted the off-box PGHOSTADDR address despite host=localhost in DSN")
    elif "localhost" in msg or "127.0.0.1" in msg or "::1" in msg:
        print("\n>>> libpq used loopback; PGHOSTADDR did NOT override")
EOF
input.description: Empirically test if PGHOSTADDR overrides loopback host in DSN

content.text: The error message is generic. Let me get a clearer signal by using a refusing/observable target and inspecting the resolved connection info, or use a port-specific failure. Let me try connecting to a PGHOSTADDR that immediately refuses (a closed port on a real reachable address won't be off-box; instead let me check via the connection object's resolution or strace-like). Better: use psycopg's internal pgconn after a failed connect to read the host/hostaddr it used.

input.command:
cd /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay && source .venv/bin/activate && python - <<'EOF'
import os
from psycopg import pq

dsn = "postgresql://localhost:5432/assay"

os.environ["PGHOSTADDR"] = "203.0.113.66"
os.environ.pop("PGHOST", None)

# Use libpq directly: PQconnectStart does NOT block; we then read what host/hostaddr it resolved.
conn = pq.PGconn.connect_start(dsn.encode())
print("hostaddr libpq resolved:", conn.hostaddr)
print("host     libpq resolved:", conn.host)
print("port     libpq resolved:", conn.port)
conn.finish()
EOF

content.content:
hostaddr libpq resolved: b'203.0.113.66'
host     libpq resolved: b'localhost'
port     libpq resolved: b'5432'

content.text:
Definitive proof. With the DSN `postgresql://localhost:5432/assay` (which passes `require_local_uri` because `host=localhost`), libpq resolves `hostaddr=203.0.113.66` from the `PGHOSTADDR` env var. libpq connects to the IP in `hostaddr` when present — `host` is then used only as the server/SSL name, not for the actual TCP connection. So graph state goes to the off-box IP `203.0.113.66` despite the guard passing.

Let me also confirm PGHOST and PGSERVICE behavior for completeness, and confirm the conn_str is passed directly to ConnectionPool (which uses libpq).

input.command:
cd /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay && source .venv/bin/activate && python - <<'EOF'
import os
from psycopg import pq

# Case A: PGHOST with a DSN that OMITS host (default would be localhost, but env supplies it)
for k in ("PGHOST","PGHOSTADDR","PGSERVICE"): os.environ.pop(k, None)
dsn_no_host = "postgresql://:5432/assay"  # no authority host
print("DSN omitting host:", dsn_no_host)
from assay_engine._local import require_local_uri, NonLocalEndpointError
try:
    require_local_uri(dsn_no_host, what="x"); print("  guard: PASS")
except NonLocalEndpointError as e:
    print("  guard: REJECT", e)

os.environ["PGHOST"] = "203.0.113.99"
c = pq.PGconn.connect_start(dsn_no_host.encode())
print("  libpq host resolved:", c.host, "hostaddr:", c.hostaddr); c.finish()
os.environ.pop("PGHOST", None)

# Case B: default DSN with PGHOST set (host=localhost explicit -> should NOT be overridden by PGHOST)
os.environ["PGHOST"] = "203.0.113.99"
c = pq.PGconn.connect_start(b"postgresql://localhost:5432/assay")
print("default DSN + PGHOST: host resolved:", c.host, "hostaddr:", c.hostaddr); c.finish()
os.environ.pop("PGHOST", None)
EOF

content.content:
DSN omitting host: postgresql://:5432/assay
  guard: REJECT x must be a local store (ADR-0003 data sovereignty); authority names non-loopback host ''
  libpq host resolved: b'203.0.113.99' hostaddr: b'203.0.113.99'
default DSN + PGHOST: host resolved: b'localhost' hostaddr: b'::1'

content.text:
The PGHOST cases confirm libpq precedence as the finding stated:
- When `host` IS explicit in the DSN (`localhost`), `PGHOST` does NOT override it (host stays `localhost`, hostaddr resolves to loopback `::1`). So PGHOST alone against the default DSN is correctly blocked by the explicit host.
- The DSN-omits-host case is rejected by the guard (empty host), so PGHOST can't fill it there.

But **PGHOSTADDR is the killer**: it populates the SEPARATE `hostaddr` parameter, which the default DSN never specifies. libpq uses `hostaddr` as the actual TCP target when present, with `host` demoted to just the SSL/auth name. This is exactly the bypass: the default conn string `postgresql://localhost:5432/assay` passes the guard, yet `PGHOSTADDR=203.0.113.66` sends graph state off-box.

Note the guard already validates a `hostaddr` parameter when it appears *in the DSN string* (lines 154, 169) — but it cannot see the env-supplied `hostaddr`, and nothing clears PGHOSTADDR/PGSERVICE. The conn_str is passed verbatim to `ConnectionPool(conn_str, ...)` which uses libpq, so the env vars are honored at connect time.

Now assessing scope: this is the data-sovereignty checkpoint guard (ADR-0003), in scope (not the out-of-scope "on-box data-sovereignty" exception — that exception refers to the design choice of being on-box, whereas this is a genuine bypass of the enforcement mechanism). It's a real, reproducible defect with a realistic threat model (an env var, e.g. set by a misconfigured systemd unit, shell profile, or a partial compromise, silently exfiltrates the entire run state). The fix would be to neutralize PGHOST/PGHOSTADDR/PGPORT/PGSERVICE (e.g., explicitly set `hostaddr` empty / pass them as explicit conn params / unset them) before opening the pool.

input.reasoning:
Confirmed real, reproducible data-sovereignty bypass (ADR-0003) in the checkpointer.

Code facts:
- get_postgres_connection_string() (checkpoint.py:157-164) resolves the DSN from ASSAY_POSTGRES_URL or the default postgresql://localhost:5432/assay, then validates it with require_local_uri.
- require_local_uri (_local.py:110-190) only inspects the DSN/URI STRING: authority hosts plus any host/hostaddr query param (URI form) or host/hostaddr DSN token (kv form). It has no knowledge of, and never clears/validates, libpq environment variables.
- grep across persistence/ and _local.py confirms nothing neutralizes PGHOST/PGHOSTADDR/PGPORT/PGSERVICE. The validated conn_str is passed verbatim to ConnectionPool(conn_str, ...) (checkpoint.py:275-287), which connects via libpq.

Empirical reproduction (psycopg 3.3.4 / libpq 18, .venv):
- With DSN "postgresql://localhost:5432/assay" (which require_local_uri PASSES, host=localhost) and PGHOSTADDR=203.0.113.66 set, pq.PGconn.connect_start resolves: host=b'localhost', hostaddr=b'203.0.113.66', port=b'5432'. libpq connects to the hostaddr IP (host is then only the SSL/auth name), so graph state goes off-box to 203.0.113.66 despite the guard passing.
- Precedence verified per the finding: with host EXPLICIT in the DSN, PGHOST does NOT override it (host stayed localhost, hostaddr resolved to ::1) — so PGHOST alone against the default DSN is blocked. The DSN-omits-host case ("postgresql://:5432/assay") is rejected by the guard outright. The genuine, unblocked bypass is PGHOSTADDR, because hostaddr is a SEPARATE libpq parameter the default DSN never specifies, so the explicit host=localhost does not shadow it; libpq fills hostaddr from PGHOSTADDR and uses it as the actual TCP target. (PGSERVICE could similarly redirect host via a service file and is likewise unhandled.)

Scope: In scope. This is a bypass of the data-sovereignty ENFORCEMENT mechanism, not the out-of-scope "on-box data-sovereignty is by-design" exception. The threat model is realistic — an env var from a shell profile, systemd unit, container env, or partial compromise silently exfiltrates the entire run state (phases, hypotheses, verdicts) that the guard exists to keep on-box. The module's own docstring (lines 4-6) asserts on-box-only enforcement, so this is a true gap against stated intent.

Remediation direction: before opening the pool, neutralize the libpq env channel — e.g. parse the DSN into explicit conn params (so host/hostaddr are fully specified and env cannot fill hostaddr), explicitly set hostaddr to the validated loopback (or empty) in kwargs, and/or strip PGHOST/PGHOSTADDR/PGPORT/PGSERVICE from the connect environment, then re-validate the fully-resolved hostaddr.

content.text: Verified SEC-10-1 as TRUE_POSITIVE. The libpq PGHOSTADDR env var genuinely bypasses the loopback guard, reproduced empirically with libpq resolving `hostaddr=203.0.113.66` from the env while the DSN `postgresql://localhost:5432/assay` passes `require_local_uri`. Relevant files: `/Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay/src/assay_engine/persistence/checkpoint.py` and `/Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay/src/assay_engine/_local.py`.
